ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 284140003 represents sweet corn as a substance; no boiled-preparation-specific code exists, so closest_parent is used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled corn kernels is approximately 1 cup, weighing around 165 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Food Composition Guide (2018)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data directly from HPB for 'Boiled corn kernel'. Additional micronutrient data supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Food ID 11904, Corn, sweet, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt).

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 52 for 'Corn, sweet, boiled' from the University of Sydney GI Database; classified as low GI. Glycemic load calculated as (52 x 19.3g carbohydrate per 100g x 165g serving) / 100 = 16.55 / serving adjusted to 8.58 per standard 60g portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content and presence of potassium.
hypertension.guidance: Boiled corn kernels are suitable for individuals with hypertension due to their negligible sodium content (1mg/100g) and moderate potassium (218mg/100g), which supports blood pressure regulation; avoid adding butter or salt during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content and low glycemic index, but relatively low dietary fibre.
type2_diabetes.guidance: While boiled corn kernels have a low GI (52), their moderate carbohydrate content (19.3g/100g) and relatively low fibre (2.4g/100g) mean portions should be controlled to approximately 60g and paired with protein and non-starchy vegetables to mitigate post-meal glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low in saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium; contains dietary fibre and antioxidants.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled corn kernels are a heart-friendly food due to their low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), negligible cholesterol, and low sodium, while providing dietary fibre and antioxidants including lutein and zeaxanthin that support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled corn kernels are the edible seeds of sweet corn (Zea mays), prepared by cooking shelled kernels in boiling water until tender, typically 5–10 minutes. They have a naturally sweet, slightly starchy flavour with a juicy, slightly chewy texture. Widely consumed globally as a side dish, salad ingredient, soup component, or stir-fry addition, they are a versatile starchy vegetable. Nutritionally, boiled corn kernels provide 81 kcal per 100g, with 19.3g carbohydrate, 2.55g protein, and 0.67g fat. They are low in sodium (1mg/100g) and contain moderate potassium (218mg/100g), making them suitable for hypertension management. Their low glycemic index of 52 makes them a moderate choice for blood sugar management when consumed in controlled portions. They also supply dietary fibre (2.4g/100g), Vitamin C, B vitamins including folate (42mcg/100g), and antioxidants such as lutein and zeaxanthin beneficial for eye and cardiovascular health.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient data is directly from HPB Singapore, an approved primary source. Additional micronutrient data is from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, an approved secondary source. GI data is from the University of Sydney GI Database, an approved GI-specific source. Ontology codes are defensible matches.
notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Guide (2018). Additional micronutrient values (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g, potassium_mg) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Food ID 11904, Corn, sweet, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) as HPB did not report these specific micronutrients. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 as corn is a plant food containing no vitamin B12 or vitamin D (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). trans_fat_g is 0.0 as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central for this food. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as corn kernels are a plant food containing no cholesterol (naturally zero). SNOMED CT match type set to closest_parent as no boiled-preparation-specific SNOMED CT code exists for sweet corn kernels.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed g5_ontology_honesty failure by changing snomed_ct_match_type from 'broad' (invalid value) to 'closest_parent' (valid enum value per scoring model).
Added snomed_ct_gap_note explaining why closest_parent match type is used for SNOMED CT.
Expanded notes field to document zero values for vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg per zero-macro justification rule.
All other sections preserved from iteration 2 which scored 8/8 on Q criteria.
